title: The Anthropic Exception: How Nvidia Lost a Key Customer and What It Learned

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
When pressed on why Anthropic—maker of one of the world's top frontier models—runs primarily on TPUs and Trainium rather than Nvidia GPUs, Jensen Huang offers a remarkably candid admission: it was his strategic miss. At the critical moment when Anthropic needed billions in investment to scale, Nvidia simply wasn't in a position to provide it. Google and AWS were, and in exchange for that capital, Anthropic committed to their compute platforms. Huang characterizes this not as a technological trend but as a singular anomaly driven by early-stage investment dynamics.

The admission cuts deeper than it first appears. Huang acknowledges that he didn't fully internalize how different building a frontier AI lab would be from a typical startup. Venture capitalists weren't going to write $5–10 billion checks on the hope that a company might become Anthropic. The hyperscalers could and did, creating a form of compute lock-in that had nothing to do with the relative merits of GPUs versus TPUs. Without Anthropic, Huang argues, there would be essentially no meaningful TPU or Trainium growth story at all.

This experience has clearly reshaped Nvidia's strategy. The company has since made massive investments—reportedly up to $30 billion in OpenAI and $10 billion in Anthropic—to ensure it never again loses a foundational AI company for lack of financial support. Huang frames these investments not as diversification plays but as ecosystem necessities: the world needs these companies to exist, and Nvidia needs them on its platform. The shift from a pure hardware supplier to an investor in its own customers represents a meaningful evolution in Nvidia's business model.

Huang is also careful to distinguish Anthropic's situation from a broader competitive threat. He repeatedly challenges competitors to demonstrate superior total cost of ownership on public benchmarks like MLPerf or Dylan Patel's InferenceMAX. No one has, he claims. And the economics don't favor switching: ASIC margins run around 65%, barely below Nvidia's roughly 70%, meaning customers aren't saving nearly as much as the narrative suggests. The real question isn't whether alternatives exist—it's whether any of them can match Nvidia's yearly cadence of order-of-magnitude improvements.

The Anthropic episode also illuminates Nvidia's broader philosophy of not picking winners. Huang draws on his own company's history—Nvidia was one of 60 graphics companies and would have topped most lists of likely failures—to justify investing broadly across the AI ecosystem rather than betting on any single lab. It's a humility born of survival, and it now shapes how Nvidia allocates billions in capital.
